learning_outcomes.description: Interpret relevant legal materials (including legislation and case law) within an Australian business law context.
Identify legal issues and potential risks arising in common business and commercial situations.
Apply core legal principles (including contract, negligence and consumer law) to analyse and resolve business-related legal problems.
Communicate legal issues using clear, structured legal methodology in plain English to a range of audiences.
Recognise professional and ethical responsibilities in business contexts, including appropriate responses to legal risk and when to seek legal advice.

assessments.task_description:
Purpose of Tutorials

Tutorials provide students with an opportunity to explore, discuss and apply the legal principles introduced in lectures. They are also designed to develop students' skills in legal reasoning, legal analysis and problem-solving in preparation for other assessment items in the Course.

There will be ten (10) one-hour Tutorials throughout the Semester. Five (5) of these Tutorials are assessable and together account for 15% of the overall Course grade.

Assessable Tutorials

The assessable Tutorials occur in Teaching Weeks 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11.

For each assessable Tutorial, students are required to prepare a written response to an ILAC-style legal problem. The purpose of these submissions is:

  1. To practice answering legal problems in preparation for other Course assessment;
  2. To further develop students' ability to apply the ILAC methodology; and
  3. To develop students' understanding of Australian Business Law.

Students are expected to attempt the problem before it is discussed during the Tutorial. The assessable Tutorials are designed to encourage preparation and active engagement with the Course material.

A general feedback document addressing common strengths, weaknesses and issues arising from each submission will be released before the next assessable Tutorial.

Submission Requirements

Students must complete and submit their Tutorial responses through Cadmus.

For each assessable Tutorial, students are required to:

  • Prepare a minimum 800-word response using;
  • Write the response using the ILAC structure (methodology);
  • Submit the response through Cadmus before 9:00am on the Monday commencing the relevant Tutorial week;
  • Attend their allocated face-to-face Tutorial; and
  • Review, refine and develop their answer during the Tutorial discussion.

Students enroll in a single face-to-face Tutorial prior to the commencement of Semester and are expected to attend their allocated class throughout the Semester.

Please note that it is the responsibility of each student to amend, refine and improve their own answer during the Tutorial class. Due to the size of the cohort and time constraints associated with the Course, detailed individual feedback on Tutorial submissions will not generally be provided.

Answers must be prepared using appropriate ILAC structure, including headings and clear signposting of legal issues, principles and application. Answers that do not use appropriate ILAC structure will not be awarded the full 3%.

Students must rely only upon Course materials when answering Tutorial questions.

Grading Criteria

Each Tutorial submission is worth a maximum of 3% of the final Course grade.

Students will receive one of the following grades for each submission:

  • 3 marks;
  • 1.5 marks; or
  • 0 marks.

To receive the full 3 marks, a submission must demonstrate a genuine and substantial attempt to answer the assigned question using the Course materials. Answers do not need to be entirely correct; however, they must demonstrate meaningful engagement with the legal issues and principles raised by the question.

Submissions that do not demonstrate a sufficient attempt to complete the task will not receive full marks.

Submissions that are less than 800 words in length will not receive 3%.

Submissions that do not strictly use the ILAC technique will not receive 3% (note: Tutorial Submission 1 does not require the use of ILAC).

Late Submission Penalties

Where a Tutorial submission is submitted after the due date, but before the due date of the next assessable Tutorial, the maximum mark available will be 1.5 marks, provided the submission is otherwise of an acceptable standard.

Any submission received after the due date of the following assessable Tutorial will receive a mark of 0, unless an approved extension has been granted.

Students who receive an approved extension must ensure that the relevant documentation is provided (via email) to their Tutor so that the extension can be applied appropriately.

Using Cadmus

LAWS1100 uses Cadmus for Tutorial assessment submissions.

Cadmus is an online assessment environment that allows students to prepare, edit and submit their written work.

Students should note the following:

  • All assessment instructions and resources will be available through Cadmus;
  • Tutorial submissions must be completed and submitted through Cadmus;
  • Students should only have Cadmus open in one browser tab or window at a time; and

For the best experience, students should use the latest version of Google Chrome.

Submission Dates

Tutorial 1 submission is due in Teaching Week 3 on: 10th August at 9.00am

Tutorial 3 submission is due in Teaching Week 5 on: 24th August at 9.00am

Tutorial 5 submission is due in Teaching Week 7 on: 7th September at 9.00am

Tutorial 7 submission is due in Teaching Week 9 on: 21st September at 9.00am

Tutorial 9 submission is due in Teaching Week 11 on: 12th October at 9.00am
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students must complete and submit their Tutorial responses through Cadmus.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: Any late submission that is subsequently submitted before the next Tutorial submission is due, will be awarded 1.5% if the work is deemed to be of acceptable quality (as outlined above).
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
26/08/2026 4:30 pm

Note: The In-Semester Examination can ONLY be accessed during the 70-minute window. Students should be ready to commence the exam promptly at the designated start time, in order to make use of the full 70-minute time allocation.
assessments.task_description:
This "In-Semester Examination" is an online examination and is weighted at 15% of your total mark for the Course.

This Exam will be held on Wednesday 26th August 2026. There will be 10 minutes perusal time and 1 hour working time to complete the Exam.

The Exam will only be accessible from 4.30pm to 5.40pm. It is your responsibility that you start and finish the exam during this period. The Exam will automatically shut off at 5.40pm.  

The Exam is scheduled during the Teaching Week 5 Wednesday lecture time. This means that ALL students in the Course must complete the Exam at this time, regardless of whether or not you are enrolled in the Wednesday lecture stream.

The Exam will consist of 5 x multiple choice questions (each worth 1%) and 2 x short answer questions (each worth 5%). The content for the Exam will be covered in the Teaching Weeks 1 & 2 lecture material, Chapters 1 - 4 of the Textbook and during Tutorial 1 and Tutorial 2.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The exam will be available on Cadmus (via Blackboard).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: Exams submitted after the end of the submission time will incur a late penalty.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
ILAC Problem Set 20%

The ILAC Problem Set is worth 20% of your final grade and is designed to provide students with an opportunity to demonstrate their ability to analyze and solve a legal problem using the ILAC methodology.

The style, structure and level of difficulty of this assessment closely reflects the style of legal problem that students can expect to encounter in both the Tutorial submissions and the End-of-Semester Examination. As such, students should approach this assessment as an important opportunity to develop and refine their legal reasoning, legal analysis and ILAC writing skills before the final examination.

The assessment will be based solely on material that has already been covered in lectures and/or Tutorials.

Assessment Details

The ILAC Problem Set will be released through the Cadmus assessment portal at:

Release: Thursday 17 September 2026 – 5:00pm

Students must complete and submit their answer through Cadmus by:

Due: Tuesday 22 September 2026 – 2:00pm

All assessment instructions, resources and submission requirements will be available within Cadmus.

Feedback

Following the release of assessment results, the problem question will be reviewed in class, and a comprehensive written feedback document will be made available on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students must complete and submit their answer through Cadmus.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
End-of-Semester Examination

The End-of-Semester Examination is worth 50% of the overall Course grade and is designed to assess students' ability to identify, analyze and resolve legal problems using the ILAC methodology.

Examination Format

The examination is an on-campus, invigilated, open-book examination that will be centrally scheduled by the University during the official Examination Period.

Students will receive:

  • 10 minutes reading and planning time; and
  • 120 minutes (2 hours) writing time.

The examination consists of two (2) ILAC-style legal problem questions, each worth 25 marks.

The questions will be similar in style and complexity to the Tutorial submissions and the ILAC Problem Set completed throughout the Semester.

Examinable Material

Students are responsible for all examinable Course materials, including:

  • Lecture PowerPoint slides;
  • Materials discussed during lectures;
  • Tutorial submission questions;
  • Tutorial discussions;
  • Tutorial PowerPoint slides and feedback documents; and
  • The prescribed textbook.

The examination covers all Course materials from Teaching Weeks 3–13.

Teaching Weeks 1 and 2 are not examinable.

Permitted Materials

The examination is open book.

Students may bring any handwritten or printed material into the examination. Materials may be annotated and annotations may be written in languages other than English.

Hard-copy dictionaries of any type are permitted.

Calculators are not permitted.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 1 - The Australian Legal System

This lecture will commence with an introduction to Business Law in Australia and discuss the main areas of law that we will study in this Course.

In particular, the first half of the lecture will provide you with an overview of the Course and identify how the law and regulations applicable to the environment in which professional accountants operate, will be spread across several weeks of the Course. This overview will also outline any significant changes that have been made recently to LAWS1100. Assessment items will also be discussed in detail.

In the second half of the lecture, we will define the law and identify important theoretical discussions surrounding the law. We will also identify the major features of the Australian Legal System - in particular providing you with a basic understanding of the Common Law and the idea of what is meant by a Federal system of Government.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

This overview will also outline any significant changes that have been made recently to LAWS1100. Assessment items will also be discussed in detail. In the second half of the lecture, we will define the law and identify important theoretical discussions surrounding the law. We will also identify the major features of the Australian Legal System - in particular providing you with a basic understanding of the Common Law and the idea of what is meant by a Federal system of Government.

learning_activities.topic:
No Tutorials

There are no Tutorials in Teaching Week 1.
learning_activities.topic_description: No Tutorials There are no Tutorials in Teaching Week 1.

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 2 - Sources of Law & the Constitution

In the first half of the lecture, we will examine the sources of Australian Law (Common Law and Legislation), the Australian Court system and the concept of Statutory Interpretation.

In the second half of the lecture, we will focus on the most important aspects of the Australian Constitution, including the powers of the Legislature, the Executive and the Judiciary, the mechanism for conflict resolution and the provision which allows for Constitutional amendment.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 3 - The ILAC Method and Tort Law (1)

This lecture will begin with an introduction to the ILAC Method. The ILAC method is the method you will use to answer all legal problems in LAWS1100. This discussion will help you to prepare for the Ungraded Problem Scenario Question that will be released on Blackboard this week and also the 2nd 3% Tutorial submission that must be submitted by 9am on the Monday of Teaching Week 5.

In the second part of this lecture, we will begin to examine the most common area of Tort Law - the Tort of Negligence. In particular, we will look at the first two requirements needed to prove a negligence claim (duty of care and breach of duty). Negligent misstatement (applicable to those providing advice such as professional accountants) will be discussed as part of the law to do with establishing a "duty of care".

The third requirement (causation) as well as potential Defences to a negligence claim, will be discussed in Teaching Week 4.

A practice Problem Question will also be uploaded to Blackboard at the beginning of Teaching Week 3. The question will be based on the material covered in the Teaching Weeks 3 and 4 lectures. During Teaching Week 3 and 4, students will have the opportunity to answer this practice question (in their own time). A model answer will then be uploaded to Blackboard so that students can compare it to their own answer. This exercise will help students prepare for the remaining 3% Tutorial submissions throughout the Semester, as well as for the 20% ILAC assessment and the 50% End-of-Semester Examination.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 4 - Tort Law (2)

We will begin the first half of this lecture, by reviewing some of the materials from Teaching Weeks 1 and 2. In particular, this session will focus on In-Semester Exam content (the In-Semester Exam will be held during the scheduled Wednesday lecture time in Teaching Week 5).

In the previous lecture, we examined the Tort of Negligence - specifically in relation to the 'Duty of Care' and 'Breach of Duty' requirements. This lecture further examines the Tort of Negligence - in particular the requirement of 'Causation' and the two possible Defences to a Negligence claim (voluntary assumption of risk and contributory negligence). 

Information on all other types of Tort actions will be recorded outside of class time and presented as a PowerPoint recording. This includes the Torts of Trespass, Defamation and Deceit.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

In the previous lecture, we examined the Tort of Negligence - specifically in relation to the 'Duty of Care' and 'Breach of Duty' requirements. This lecture further examines the Tort of Negligence - in particular the requirement of 'Causation' and the two possible Defences to a Negligence claim (voluntary assumption of risk and contributory negligence). Information on all other types of Tort actions will be recorded outside of class time and presented as a PowerPoint recording . This includes the Torts of Trespass, Defamation and Deceit.

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 5 - Contract Law (1)

All students in the Course must sit the In-Semester Exam during the Wednesday lecture time on the 26th August (this includes students enrolled in the Tuesday lecture).

For equality purposes, the material for this week will therefore be recorded for all students. NOTE: there will be NO in person Tuesday or Wednesday lecture steams.

This week, we will commence our 3 week study of Contract Law. We will focus on the primary elements involved in the formation of a Contract.

The 3 major requirements are:

1.) agreement;

2.) intention and

3.) consideration. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 6 - Contract Law (2)

In this lecture, we will examine Contract Law in greater detail. In last week's recording, we looked at the 3 requirements needed to form a valid legal contract. This week, we will study the law surrounding the contract itself (after the contract has been formed).

In particular we will identify what does and does not constitute a term of a contract (including how the Parole Evidence Rule affects this). We will also examine other important areas of Contract Law such as promissory estoppel and contractual capacity.

The law to do with disclaimers/exclusion clauses will be discussed at the beginning of the Teaching Week 7 lecture.

Please note: Contract Law 2 often challenges students in terms of legal content. It is very important that you attend the Tutorial on Contract Law 2 (held in Teaching Week 8) if you wish to gain a better understanding of the material.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

We will also examine other important areas of Contract Law such as promissory estoppel and contractual capacity. The law to do with disclaimers/exclusion clauses will be discussed at the beginning of the Teaching Week 7 lecture. Please note: Contract Law 2 often challenges students in terms of legal content. It is very important that you attend the Tutorial on Contract Law 2 (held in Teaching Week 8) if you wish to gain a better understanding of the material.

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 7 - Contract Law (3)

This lecture will commence with an analysis of a question based on the law to do with disclaimers/exclusion clauses (an area mentioned at the end of the Teaching Week 6 material).

Various answers to the question will be provided, ranging from high quality ILAC answers to low quality ILAC answers. This exercise will help you prepare for the upcoming 20% ILAC Problem Scenario Exercise.

For the main part of this lecture, we will examine the 5 Vitiating Factors that can lead to a contract being terminated for lack of free and voluntary consent.

The 5 vitiating factors are: misrepresentation, mistake, undue influence, duress and unconscionable conduct. These vitiating factors will also be discussed in the context of how they may apply to the advice given by professionals such as accountants.

Contract Law remedies will also be discussed, as will the ways in which a contract can be brought to an end.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 8 - Intellectual Property Law

At the start of the lecture, you will be delivered a presentation on the upcoming 20% ILAC Problem Set Scenario assessment task.

We will then investigate Intellectual Property (IP) Law. Our main focus will be on Copyright law (both in terms of Copyright protection and Copyright infringement).

In addition to Copyright Law, a brief overview of other areas of Intellectual Property (including Trademarks, Patents, Design and Commercial Secret) will also be provided.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

In addition to Copyright Law, a brief overview of other areas of Intellectual Property (including Trademarks, Patents, Design and Commercial Secret) will also be provided.

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 9 - Competition and Consumer Law (1)

This week, we will commence with our 2 week study of the Competition and Consumer Act. The lectures will be divided into two main areas: the first focusing on a Schedule to the Competition and Consumer Act called the ACL (Australian Consumer Law) and the second focusing on anti-competitive provisions under the Act itself.

This week, we will examine the areas of Misleading and Deceptive Conduct, Unconscionable Conduct, Unfair Terms, False Representations and Implied Consumer Guarantees, that fall under the Australian Consumer Law (ACL) schedule.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

This week, we will examine the areas of Misleading and Deceptive Conduct, Unconscionable Conduct, Unfair Terms, False Representations and Implied Consumer Guarantees, that fall under the Australian Consumer Law (ACL) schedule.

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 10 - Competition and Consumer Law (2)

This is the second lecture in which we learn about the Competition and Consumer Act. In the first half of the lecture, we will finish examining the areas of Misleading and Deceptive Conduct, Unconscionable Conduct, Unfair Terms, False Representations and Implied Consumer Guarantees, that fall under the Australian Consumer Law (ACL) schedule.

In the second half of the lecture, we will then discuss many anti-competitive behaviour provisions that are located under the Competition and Consumer Act 2010. These provisions target behaviour that leads to substantially lessening competition in the marketplace.

Remedies available under the Competition and Consumer Act will also be discussed at the end of this lecture.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 11 - The Law of Agency

In this lecture, we will examine the law to do with Principals and their Agents and the special relationship that exists between them.

The fiduciary duty that an Agent owes to his/her Principal will also be discussed.

We will conclude the lecture by analysing a question on the Law of Agency. In this Course, the Law of Agency is discussed in the lecture material, but is not part of the Tutorial syllabus. Therefore, a Tutorial style question will be answered during this lecture.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 12 - Business Structures

In this lecture, we will look at the different types of key business structures in Australia including Sole Traders, Partnerships and Corporations. The main focus of this discussion will be on the area of 'partnerships', which is closely related to the Law of Agency.

This lecture is also aimed at further improving your ability to answer legal questions using the ILAC technique, in preparation for the End-of-Semester Examination. An interactive discussion will occur, based upon the 20% ILAC Problem Set Scenario Exercise. General feedback for this assessment item will also be provided throughout this discussion.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Tutorial 10 - Competition and Consumer Law

In this Tutorial, students will attempt to answer a practice ILAC question based on the material taught in the Teaching Week 10 lecture.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Tutorial 10 -  Competition and Consumer Law In this Tutorial, students will attempt to answer a practice ILAC question based on the material taught in the Teaching Week 10 lecture.

learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Wk 13 - Revision and Exam Preparation

This week, you will be provided with all of the details to do with the End-of-semester Examination. You will also be provided with a brief summary and overview of all the material that has been covered throughout the Semester. All students in the Course must listen to the recording of this lecture well before the End-of-Semester Examination takes place.

This lecture will be recorded on Zoom and take approximately 1 hour.

Please note: there are no formal Tuesday or Wednesday lectures in Teaching Week 13.
